main_summary.summary: William "Willie" S. Leventhal, born in New York in 1946 and raised in Los Angeles, was interviewed on April 13 (year unstated) in Malibu, California, by David Cline for the Southern Oral History Program, the Smithsonian NMAAHC, and the Library of Congress. Leventhal recounts a Jewish upbringing in the Ramona Gardens federal housing project and the South Bay, where exposure to antisemitic slurs and the Jackie Robinson story shaped his early racial consciousness. As a UCLA freshman, he answered Martin Luther King Jr.'s on-campus February 1965 recruiting call for the SCOPE (Summer Community Organization and Political Education) project organized by Hosea Williams. After orientation at Morehouse College alongside Bayard Rustin, A. Philip Randolph, C. Vann Woodward, John Hope Franklin, Septima Clark, Vernon E. Jordan Jr., Charles Morgan Jr., Clarence Mitchell Jr., and John Doar, he was assigned to Bibb County, Georgia, working under William P. Randall in Macon. He was arrested twice in Macon, then arrested with John R. Lewis in Americus while attempting to integrate the First Methodist Church the week after Andrew Whatley was killed. He was shot at twice in Americus, stayed past the project's end for the Reverend Marcus Moon water-commissioner election in Macon, and later served as SCLC Western Bureau college coordinator. He recounts a 112-day Los Angeles County jail incarceration following a Santa Monica gun-purchase incident and conflict with police in fall 1966, during which jail psychiatrist Marcus Crahan diagnosed him as unstable.
key_themes: Hosea Williams's leadership and battle-plan organizing
Americus, Georgia and Andrew Whatley's killing
main_summary.historical_significance:
Leventhal's account preserves an extensively detailed first-person record of SCLC's SCOPE project, a 1965 summer voter-registration effort that recruited 500 of a planned 2,000 volunteers into 90 of 120 targeted counties across the rural South. He documents the orientation week at Morehouse College and the SCLC-SNCC tensions, names specific co-workers across Macon and Americus host families and local leadership (William P. Randall, Tom Jackson, the Simmons family, Sammy Mahone, Gloria Wise), and provides eyewitness testimony to the week Andrew Whatley was killed, including church-integration arrests with John R. Lewis at the hands of Sheriff Fred B. Chappell.

The interview also functions as a sustained critique of how the published civil rights historiography of David J. Garrow and Taylor Branch, in Leventhal's view, weaponized FBI surveillance records and erased SCOPE from the canonical narrative. He details his own work on the federal report to President Reagan and Congress for Coretta Scott King on the first King Holiday under Lloyd Davis, and traces post-1965 SCLC Western Bureau activity (Operation Breadbasket, the Western Christian Leadership Conference under Rev. Thomas Kilgore Jr. and Gwen Green) that has rarely been documented elsewhere.

chapters.title: Childhood and Early Identity
chapters.summary: Leventhal describes his birth in New York in 1946, the family's move to the Ramona Gardens federal housing project in Los Angeles, his parents' NAACP involvement, and his Jewish grandfather's donations to the Scottsboro Boys defense. He recounts encounters with the slur 'kike' in South Bay Catholic neighborhoods and the formative impact of the Jackie Robinson Story film at age five.

chapters.title: Jewish Athletes and Kennedy
chapters.summary: Leventhal discusses his religious confirmation, his identification with Jewish athletes Hank Greenberg, Sandy Koufax, and Al Rosen, and his admiration for John F. Kennedy as the New Frontier figure. He recalls working out with the Brooklyn Dodgers at the Coliseum at ages 13 and 14 under Pete Reiser and Gil Hodges.

chapters.title: Decision to Join SCOPE
chapters.summary: Leventhal recounts hearing of the Selma campaign and Bloody Sunday during his UCLA freshman year, Dr. King's on-campus recruiting visit for the Summer Community Organization and Political Education (SCOPE) project, and his decision to volunteer despite his fear, prompted by another Jewish friend from his high school football team.

chapters.title: Morehouse Orientation Week
chapters.summary: Leventhal describes the SCOPE orientation at Morehouse College in June 1965, naming the lineup of speakers: Bayard Rustin, A. Philip Randolph, Hosea Williams, C. Vann Woodward, John Hope Franklin, Lillian Smith, Septima Clark, Vernon E. Jordan Jr., Charles Morgan Jr., Clarence Mitchell Jr., Michael Harrington, and John Doar. He recalls Dr. King's closing speech invoking Muslims and Hindus alongside Jews and Gentiles.

chapters.title: Macon Assignment and Bibb Bombers
chapters.summary: Leventhal recounts his Bibb County, Georgia assignment under William P. Randall's leadership in Macon, lodging with the Simmons family in East Macon, and integrating the Macon Bombers semi-pro Black baseball team. He describes the team's road games, his at-bat statistics, and the team's traveling bread truck.

chapters.title: Macon Arrests
chapters.summary: Leventhal describes his first arrest in Macon while telephoning from a barbershop after a colleague's car was towed, William P. Randall's intervention with the police chief, and a second arrest after letting William P. Randall's nephew William C. Randall drive without a license. He notes the Macon office's later transformation into Phil Walden's Capricorn Records, home to The Allman Brothers.

chapters.title: Americus and Andrew Whatley's Death
chapters.summary: Leventhal describes being summoned to Americus, Georgia after Andrew Whatley was killed, with Dick Gregory in town, four women in jail, and night marches led by Hosea Williams. He recounts attempting to integrate the First Baptist Church with John R. Lewis and quoting scripture to fire chief Henderson, leading the next Sunday's group to the First Methodist Church when Willie Bolden could not be arrested, and being booked by Sheriff Fred B. Chappell.

chapters.title: Shot At in Americus
chapters.summary: Leventhal recounts attempting to organize a maids' union with SNCC worker Rich Adler, being shot at twice at close range from a pickup truck while driving Fred Hoffman's distinctive Citroen, the FBI's initial refusal to investigate Bill Raoul's disappearance, and the resulting post-traumatic stress that lasted decades.

chapters.title: Macon Election and Threat
chapters.summary: Leventhal recounts staying three weeks past SCOPE's August 28 end date to work the September 15 Macon water-commissioner election for Rev. Marcus Moon, the morning-of polling place changes designed to suppress turnout, the anonymous phone threat that gave him 24 hours to leave town, and William P. Randall's insistence that he depart by air the following morning.

chapters.title: Meeting Dr. King and SCLC Staff Service
chapters.summary: Leventhal recounts the end-of-project staff meeting at West Hunter Street Baptist Church, where Dr. King personally explained his anti-Vietnam War position to the new SCLC field staff. He describes Dr. King as warm and accessible in small-group settings, contrasting with the orator-on-stage image, and discusses his own critique of Taylor Branch and David J. Garrow's reliance on FBI surveillance records.

chapters.title: Western Bureau College Coordinator
chapters.summary: Leventhal describes returning to UCLA and being named college coordinator at the Western Christian Leadership Conference (WCLC) at 2400 Southwestern Avenue under Rev. Thomas Kilgore Jr. and executive director Gwen Green. He recounts organizing an Operation Breadbasket campaign in Los Angeles in parallel with Jesse Jackson's Chicago work, and bringing James Bevel to speak.

chapters.title: Santa Monica Arrest and 112 Days in LA County Jail
chapters.summary: Leventhal recounts a fall 1966 incident in Santa Monica when a landlord's bomb threat against him for hosting Black children led him to purchase a shotgun, an altercation with Santa Monica police, his arrest, jail psychiatrist Marcus Crahan's diagnostic report, a 112-day incarceration in LA County Jail (where he was attacked by Nation of Islam inmate Hugo Maxie), and his eventual release after a plea bargain.

chapters.title: Qualified Immunity and Closing
chapters.summary: Leventhal closes with an account of a later Iowa civil rights case where he sued a state trooper, the Supreme Court declining the appeal, and his critique of arguable probable cause and qualified immunity as the legal mechanism producing wrongful convictions that the Innocence Project addresses.
related_events: Des Moines Register bike ride incident

loc_video.caption: William S. Leventhal oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in El Segundo, California, 2013 April 13
